response:
Here’s a clear comparison between **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** in a table format:

| **Feature**               | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**                          | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**                          |
|---------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| **Type**                  | Connection-oriented (requires handshake)                       | Connectionless (no handshake)                            |
| **Reliability**           | Reliable (guarantees delivery, ordered packets)                | Unreliable (no guarantee of delivery or order)           |
| **Error Checking**        | Yes (checksum + acknowledgments + retransmissions)             | Yes (checksum only, no retransmissions)                  |
| **Flow Control**          | Yes (adjusts transmission speed to avoid congestion)           | No (sends data at full speed)                            |
| **Congestion Control**    | Yes (adjusts transmission rate to network conditions)          | No (no congestion control)                               |
| **Packet Ordering**       | Guaranteed (packets arrive in the same order they were sent)   | Not guaranteed (packets may arrive out of order)          |
| **Speed**                 | Slower (due to overhead from reliability mechanisms)           | Faster (minimal overhead)                                |
| **Header Size**           | 20–60 bytes (larger due to control flags)                      | 8 bytes (smaller, simpler structure)                     |
| **Use Cases**             | Web browsing (HTTP/HTTPS), email (SMTP), file transfers (FTP), databases | Video streaming (YouTube, Netflix), VoIP (Zoom, Skype), online gaming, DNS |
| **Overhead**              | High (due to connection setup, acknowledgments, retransmissions)| Low (no connection setup or teardown)                     |
| **Connection Establishment** | Requires **3-way handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)            | No handshake (just sends data)                           |
| **Connection Termination** | Requires **4-way handshake** (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK)             | No termination (connectionless)                          |
| **Multiplexing**          | Supports multiple applications via **ports**                  | Also supports ports (but no connection tracking)
